author: @stevecummins324
text:
If the bearings and end pieces  are rigid shouldn't be too difficult to remove warp by squaring the ends up, *as if* it were on a piece on a lathe. Just need
1) way of turning the drum.... (belt drive running around drum to pulley on a motor on a hinged and weighted plate? Rubber sheet on a drill  drum sanding adapter might drive a drum?  Mechanism of a  "Bungy cord pole lathe" could work equally well but cutting takes a bit more skill as direction rotation oscillates 
2)some kind of  "bar" that can be moved and then held rigidly horizontally near the drum's axis to serve as place to lever tool against/be a rough visual gauge as to how smoothing progressing 
3) sureform, plane or chisel... With an extra long handle  attached to it, for smoothing.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
Could likely use "drum and castors" to make a lathe headstock out of an old car wheel. Drill+ tap 4 tangential holes in wheel... And add bolts through those holes, with heads to outside ... And that's a simple 4 jaw independent chuck. Duplication of such could work as a tailstock. Add a bed and a tool post to slide on it and that's a functional wood lathe. 
Metal lathe would be a development of such.

author: @MiscDotGeek
text: I'd love to see what this could do at the peak of a roof on even a small house. The roof will collect a lot of wind and drive it to the top and power it even stronger than the base wind speed. A firm mounting would be needed. You could also vary the distance from the magnets to the transformer to vary the load, even automatically with some work.

author: @michaeljanssens1692
text: If I had two coils of wire and some magnets for a generator. Can I wire the coils in series like batteries and the voltage be added together.1.5v+1.5v=3v. Let me know

replies.author: @ronaldd2154
replies.text:
Yes. That is the idea. Generally more coil length you have or add the higher your voltage will be. Thats why you notice in things like transformers which convert from low voltages <20v to several hundreds of volts. Next is your wire size. Thicker wire of same length collects / gives out more amps, less volts. Thinner wire, less amps more volts. Look at it like water pressure from pipes or blowing on thin straw vs thicker straw. 

Its a juggling act between how much wire length you need, what size thickness, how many coils you need, and to what expectations of output your design should produce.

Too much, or too little when designing wind turbines is no good. If your turbine is producing much greater energy than what your coil setup can handle it could easily overheat/melt. Of course we know of this and are ways around it. The other being it won't start. If your generator is too big for your design, your turbine won't have enough power except in hurricanes to push/start it. (this is all extreme examples/ black and white for contrast). Look up coil wiring on youtube for wind turbines. Theres plenty of vids to give you a ballpark /idea of what to expect.

author: @darrellpidgeon6440
text: I think this is wild!  Simple scraps to demo a principle.  The magnets at the rim are the best idea.  Reminds me of a demo I did for a friend, using a record album.  I put a piece of tape halfway out from the spindle, and a piece at the edge.  I asked which piece was moving faster.  He said they were moving the same.  I only smiled and explained the difference between rpm and speed.  Both pieces move the same rpm, but the piece on the rim moves twice the distance in the same time.   Therefore that piece is moving faster.  It only took a couple of seconds for the guy to grasp the concept.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
Suggestion to anybody who has the time and motivation: get loads and loads of those small pow voltage power transformers used for small electronic devices, find a much of them that are of the same general power input and output, and mount them to a ring that has a lot of smaller magnets [because of how magnetic fields cross over the coils] and this should give very smooth, flicker-free DC voltage if you use an out-of-phase array, or, fairly consistent AC power if you set them up to operate in a 3 phase array. ~( 'w')/

Some places literally throw those little portable chargers out any more since so few of them re even actually used once the original item they came with is discarded, so you might even be able to go around asking your family, friends and neighbors for any they may have sitting in a junk drawer, in an old shoe box, or just laying around gathering dust on a shelf in their garage.

If one is resourceful, they should be able to get hundreds of them and be able to set up quite the rig, or, perhaps several of them, it mainly depends on how many coils you aim to apply to each turbine, and just divide your pile of loot by the number of coils you need per turbine and make that many turbines.

       /)/) 
    (  'ˬ') 
@(>_ )-b 
     \) \)

One idea occurred to me, however, as to how you could still get a "true" surface on the drum - place the drum on its side, make a ring form around the edge and pour maybe 3/8" of resin.  Let gravity work to level it off and (once it hardens) fix the magnets to that.  😊

author: @kenrowe167
text:
You are right, Rob, you don't need high torque. Power out from the rotor = torque (nm) x angular velocity (rad/sec). So a torque of 10 nm at an angular velocity of 1 rad/sec will yield 10 watts. Likewise, a torque of 1 nm at an angular velocity of 10 rad/sec will also yield 10 watts. So, high torque and low speed or low torque and high speed - it's the same thing.
Ignore the detractors, Rob. I can't say that I always find your videos practical, but I always find them interesting. Keep it up.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate and I am unashamed about the vids not always being practical - quite often things just don't work - but I am ok with that - what I think is important is that you try - cheers

replies.author: @jasonwitt8619
replies.text: Oh and a tip on making it true as you said. I do a lot with wood and on this I would take the magnets off and spin it with a drill off the edge of a table, just clamp the drill down onto the table and put a zip tie on the trigger when ready. Next I would lay my router sideways on the table and start the drill then sneak up on the wood with the router cutting bit. Keeping in mind the only area that would need to be 100% true is the area that the magnets get glued too. That would solve that issue real quick. That is the quick way to lathe this with tools you already have at hand. I would leave it on the base that it is already on while doing the lathe and let the drill just do all the work on turning the axle.

author: @Martyr217
text: What diameter are old speed limit signs? Even if they are warped you can at least heat and bend the metal unlike wood, also thin and lightweight.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - like that mate - I might get into a little trouble if I went around 'collecting' a few lol

author: @clint9344
text: Interesting once again Robert. If I could suggest, I would try and use magnetic bearing for support on the vertical if possible. This would give you a floating turbine and not require much force or torque to turn the turbine. I am researching and playing around with this idea myself.  Keep up the great work,  love the ideas and the comprehendable explanations  God speed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like the idea mate and in a really good version it would be well worth it - but for this - the expense and extra complexity probably wouldn't really be worth it - it's all about balance - pun not intended lol

author: @seamuscharles9028
text: Magnets and a transformer on the outher rim as well

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: fors ure

author: @garyhardman8369
text:
I've only been a subscriber for a couple of months. Still have not been able to work out what the 'FWG' stands for in your opening sequence.
Is it me being thick?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - no mate it's forward working group - the idea is we work together to make things happen in the future

replies.author: @garyhardman8369
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  Thank you. Good to know that my senility is not as advanced as I thought!

author: @patricklamberson6969
text: Magnets around the outside of the rim.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: go for it- I was worried about fixing them firmly - but it was only my solution to that issue

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
G'day again Rob, the outside is warped and the blades are also inconsistent with varying degrees of angles, yet it worked and worked well. 
While making it true, imagine if it was on a rotatable base with a blade out back like a windmill to keep it in the wind. Also a second transformer the same connected to the first in parallel with a capacitor bank and rectifier, then the light panel would stay on constantly. 👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I know mate - it was a pile of garbage lol - it's amazing it did anything

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering the best prototypes are rubbish that do well to provide proof of concept.

author: @hommerdalor6301
text: Hi Rob. put the exercise bike 90 degrees, you'll get vertical axis. Cheer's.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sur mate - but most of those bearings aren't thrust bearings so it probably won't really cope too well - or at least that's what I was thinking

author: @jamest.5001
text: How about trying 'super charging' the magnets, by adding a smaller neodymium magnet to the ceramic magnet???    It could possibly add a bit of performance to the generator,  I have heard a speaker company did this, I just wonder if it would be worth the cost and effort in a generator!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah I wonder that too mate - I am going towards a kWh genie and I want it cheap lol
